To find the probability that a student from Group B has higher engagement than one from Group A we define the difference variable D as X_B minus X_A. Since both variables are normally distributed the difference D is also normally distributed. The mean of D equals 75 minus 70 which is 5. The variance of D equals 10 squared plus 12 squared which is 244. The standard deviation of D is the square root of 244 or approximately 15.62. We need the probability that D is greater than 0. We calculate the z-score as 0 minus 5 divided by 15.62 which equals approximately -0.32. Looking up this z-score in a standard normal table gives a cumulative probability of about 0.3745. The probability that D is greater than 0 is 1 minus 0.3745 which equals 0.6255. Therefore there is roughly a 62.55 percent chance that a random student from Group B has higher engagement.

For the performance improvement part we set the exponential function equal to the logistic function to find the time t. The equation is 50 times e to the power of 0.02t equals 100 divided by 1 plus e to the power of -0.15 times (t minus 5). Solving this equation requires numerical methods because an algebraic solution is not straightforward. An explanation of the process shows that at t equals 5 the logistic value is 50 while the exponential value is about 55. At t equals 10 the logistic value is about 73 while the exponential value is about 61. The curves cross between these two points. Using a numerical solver we find the intersection occurs at approximately t equals 7.4. This force of growth models shows how different learning paths converge. There is nothing in the math that prevents this intersection from happening. The process of finding this time involves iterative calculation to match the values from both models.
